title: Supervised Learning
description: Foundations of supervised learning: linear models, trees, ensemble methods, SVMs, and instance-based learning with FAANG-depth intuition and practical code.
details: Supervised learning maps inputs X to outputs Y using labeled training data. This file covers the essential algorithms: linear regression (OLS, gradient descent), regularized regression (Ridge, Lasso, ElasticNet), logistic regression for classification, decision trees, random forests and gradient boosting (XGBoost/LightGBM/CatBoost), support vector machines with the kernel trick, and K-nearest neighbors. Each section includes the mathematical formulation, intuition, practical hyperparameter tuning, and FAANG interview questions.

sections.title: Linear Regression
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Fitting the best line through points by minimizing squared vertical distances. The OLS estimator has a closed form: ŵ = (XᵀX)⁻¹Xᵀy.

Key concepts:

  • Normal equation: ŵ = (XᵀX)⁻¹Xᵀy — O(n³) for inversion, use for n < 10K
  • Gradient descent: w := w - α·(2/n)·Xᵀ(Xw - y) — iterative, scales to large n
  • R²: proportion of variance explained. R² = 1 - SS_res/SS_tot
  • F-statistic: tests whether at least one predictor is significant
  • Multicollinearity: correlated predictors inflate coefficient variance (check VIF > 10)

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: Predicting house prices from sq ft (X) and bedrooms. OLS gives w₁ = $150/sqft, w₂ = $15K/bedroom, intercept = -$50K. R² = 0.78, meaning 78% of price variance is explained by these two features.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression

# OLS closed form
X_b = np.c_[np.ones((X.shape[0], 1)), X]  # add bias term
w = np.linalg.inv(X_b.T @ X_b) @ X_b.T @ y
print(f'Weights: {w}')

# sklearn
model = LinearRegression()
model.fit(X, y)
print(f'Coef: {model.coef_}, Intercept: {model.intercept_}')

# Manual gradient descent
def gradient_descent(X, y, lr=0.01, epochs=1000):
    m, n = X.shape
    w = np.zeros((n, 1))
    for _ in range(epochs):
        grad = (2/m) * X.T @ (X @ w - y.reshape(-1, 1))
        w -= lr * grad
    return w

sections.title: Regularized Regression (Ridge, Lasso, ElasticNet)
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: L1 gives sparsity because diamond constraint hits axes. L2's circle never does. Lasso actively selects features. Ridge shrinks coefficients but keeps all.

Formulas:

  • Ridge: L = Σ(y - ŷ)² + λ·Σwⱼ² — closed form: (XᵀX + λI)⁻¹Xᵀy
  • Lasso: L = Σ(y - ŷ)² + λ·Σ|wⱼ| — no closed form, use coordinate descent
  • ElasticNet: L = MSE + α·λ·Σ|w| + (1-α)·λ·½·Σw² — combines both

[Practical Workflow] λ tuning: Use cross-validation. RidgeCV, LassoCV, ElasticNetCV find optimal λ automatically. As λ → 0, get OLS. As λ → ∞, coefficients → 0.
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.linear_model import RidgeCV, LassoCV, ElasticNetCV
import numpy as np

# Ridge with CV
alphas = np.logspace(-3, 3, 20)
ridge = RidgeCV(alphas=alphas, cv=5).fit(X, y)
print(f'Best ridge alpha: {ridge.alpha_:.4f}')

# Lasso (L1) — sparse coefficients
lasso = LassoCV(alphas=alphas, cv=5, max_iter=10000).fit(X, y)
print(f'Non-zero coefs: {np.sum(lasso.coef_ != 0)}')

# ElasticNet — mix of L1+L2
elastic = ElasticNetCV(
    alphas=alphas, l1_ratio=[.1, .5, .7, .9, .95, .99, 1],
    cv=5
).fit(X, y)
print(f'Best alpha: {elastic.alpha_:.4f}, l1_ratio: {elastic.l1_ratio_:.2f}')

sections.title: Logistic Regression
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Linear model squashed through a sigmoid to give a probability. The decision boundary is still linear (w·x + b = 0).

Key concepts:

  • Log-odds: log(p/(1-p)) = w·x + b — the linear model predicts log-odds, and the sigmoid maps to [0, 1]
  • MLE derivation: Maximize Π σ(w·xᵢ)ʸⁱ·(1-σ(w·xᵢ))¹⁻ʸⁱ → minimize cross-entropy loss
  • Multiclass: One-vs-Rest (OvR, k binary classifiers) vs Softmax (multinomial logistic, single model with k outputs)

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: Email spam classifier. w·x = 2.3 (log-odds). σ(2.3) = 0.91 → 91% probability of spam. Threshold at 0.5 classifies as spam.
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression

# Binary classification
model = LogisticRegression(C=1.0, solver='lbfgs', max_iter=1000)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)
preds = model.predict(X_test)
probas = model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]  # P(y=1)

# Multiclass: softmax (multinomial)
multi = LogisticRegression(
    C=1.0, multi_class='multinomial',
    solver='lbfgs', max_iter=1000
)
multi.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Coefficients tell you feature importance
print(f'Coefficients: {model.coef_}')
print(f'Intercept: {model.intercept_}')

sections.title: Decision Trees
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Recursively split data into purest subsets — like playing 20 Questions where each question divides the remaining possibilities most evenly.

Split criteria:

  • Gini impurity: G = 1 - Σpⱼ². Range [0, 1-1/k]. Fast, practically identical to entropy.
  • Entropy: H = -Σpⱼ·log₂(pⱼ). Slightly slower. Gives same splits in practice.
  • Information gain: IG = H(parent) - Σ(nᵢ/n)·H(childᵢ)

Pruning: Use ccp_alpha (cost-complexity pruning). Also limit: max_depth, min_samples_leaf, min_samples_split.
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier, plot_tree
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Train with pruning
model = DecisionTreeClassifier(
    max_depth=5,
    min_samples_leaf=10,
    min_samples_split=20,
    ccp_alpha=0.001  # cost-complexity pruning
)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Visualize
plt.figure(figsize=(20, 10))
plot_tree(model, filled=True, feature_names=feature_names,
          class_names=class_names)
plt.show()

# Feature importance
for name, imp in zip(feature_names, model.feature_importances_):
    print(f'{name}: {imp:.3f}')

sections.title: Random Forest
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Train many high-variance trees on bootstrapped data, average them to reduce variance without increasing bias. Each tree is deep and overfits; the ensemble doesn't.

Two sources of randomness:

  • Bagging: Each tree trained on bootstrap sample (n samples with replacement). ~63% of unique samples per tree.
  • Feature subspace: Each split considers only sqrt(p) features (classification) or p/3 (regression). This decorrelates trees.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: 500 trees, each with 63% of data, sqrt(100 features) = 10 features per split. OOB score = 0.84 (out-of-bag = built-in validation). Adding more trees beyond ~200 has diminishing returns.
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier

model = RandomForestClassifier(
    n_estimators=300,
    max_features='sqrt',      # for classification
    max_depth=None,           # grow full trees
    min_samples_leaf=1,
    oob_score=True,           # out-of-bag evaluation
    n_jobs=-1,
    random_state=42
)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)
print(f'OOB Score: {model.oob_score_:.3f}')
print(f'Test Score: {model.score(X_test, y_test):.3f}')

# Feature importance
importances = pd.Series(
    model.feature_importances_,
    index=feature_names
).sort_values(ascending=False)

sections.title: Gradient Boosting (XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost)
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Sequential trees where each new tree corrects the residual errors of all previous trees combined. Unlike Random Forest (parallel), boosting is sequential — each tree learns from the mistakes of the ensemble.

Three implementations:

  • XGBoost: Level-wise growth, built-in L1/L2 regularization (unique!), handles missing values natively. Default choice for tabular data.
  • LightGBM: Leaf-wise growth (faster, can overfit with small data), histogram-based splits, GOSS (gradient-based one-side sampling). Much faster than XGBoost on large data.
  • CatBoost: Handles categorical features natively (ordered target encoding), uses symmetric trees. Use when you have many categorical features.

[Practical Workflow] Workflow: Start with XGBoost default params. Tune: n_estimators (early stopping), learning_rate, max_depth, subsample, colsample_bytree. Switch to LightGBM if data > 100K rows. Switch to CatBoost if > 10 categorical features.
sections.codeBlock:
import xgboost as xgb
import lightgbm as lgb
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingClassifier

# XGBoost
model = xgb.XGBClassifier(
    n_estimators=1000, learning_rate=0.05,
    max_depth=6, subsample=0.8,
    colsample_bytree=0.8,
    reg_lambda=1.0,  # L2 regularization
    reg_alpha=0.0,    # L1 regularization
    early_stopping_rounds=50,
    eval_metric='logloss'
)
model.fit(X_train, y_train, eval_set=[(X_val, y_val)], verbose=False)

# LightGBM
model = lgb.LGBMClassifier(
    n_estimators=1000, learning_rate=0.05,
    num_leaves=31, subsample=0.8,
    colsample_bytree=0.8,
    verbosity=-1
)
model.fit(X_train, y_train, eval_set=[(X_val, y_val)])

sections.title: Support Vector Machines (SVM)
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Find the hyperplane that maximizes the margin between classes. The support vectors are the hardest to classify points — they define the decision boundary.

Key concepts:

  • Kernel trick: Replace dot product xᵢ·xⱼ with K(xᵢ,xⱼ) = φ(xᵢ)·φ(xⱼ). Never compute φ explicitly.
  • RBF kernel: K(x,y) = exp(-γ·||x-y||²). Maps to infinite-dimensional space. γ controls the radius of influence.
  • C parameter: Penalty for misclassifications. Small C → soft margin (more tolerates errors). Large C → hard margin (overfits).
  • Dual formulation: L = Σαᵢ - ½·Σαᵢαⱼyᵢyⱼ·K(xᵢ,xⱼ). Only depends on dot products — enables the kernel trick.
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.svm import SVC

# RBF kernel — most common, single γ to tune
model = SVC(kernel='rbf', C=1.0, gamma='scale', probability=True)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Hyperparameter tuning
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV

param_grid = {
    'C': [0.1, 1, 10, 100],
    'gamma': ['scale', 'auto', 0.01, 0.1, 1],
    'kernel': ['rbf']
}
grid = GridSearchCV(SVC(), param_grid, cv=5, scoring='accuracy')
grid.fit(X_train_scaled, y_train)  # ALWAYS scale for SVM!
print(f'Best params: {grid.best_params_}')

sections.title: K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: A lazy learner — there's no training phase. The decision is made at prediction time by looking at the k closest training examples and voting. The shape of the decision boundary is entirely data-driven.

Critical considerations:

  • Distance metrics: Euclidean (default), Manhattan (high-dim), Cosine (text), Minkowski (generalized). Must scale features — otherwise features with larger ranges dominate.
  • k selection: Small k → low bias, high variance (overfits). Large k → smoother boundary. Rule of thumb: k ≈ √n. Use elbow on validation error.
  • Weighted voting: Inverse distance weighting — closer neighbors have more say. Often improves performance.
  • Curse of dimensionality: In high dimensions, all points are far apart — distance becomes meaningless. Use dimensionality reduction first.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: 10K samples, 20 features. k=5 (default). With 100 features, performance drops 15% due to curse of dimensionality — apply PCA first to reduce to 20 components.
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsClassifier
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

# Always scale for KNN — distance-based!
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_train_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)
X_test_scaled = scaler.transform(X_test)

model = KNeighborsClassifier(
    n_neighbors=5,
    weights='distance',   # inverse distance weighting
    metric='minkowski',   # default = Euclidean
    p=2                   # L2 norm
)
model.fit(X_train_scaled, y_train)

# Find optimal k via cross-validation
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score

for k in [3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 15, 21, 31]:
    knn = KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=k)
    scores = cross_val_score(knn, X_train_scaled, y_train, cv=5)
    print(f'k={k}: {scores.mean():.3f} +/- {scores.std():.3f}')

sections.title: Time Series Forecasting — Temporal Patterns & Autocorrelation
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Time series is different from standard supervised learning because the data has temporal structure — today depends on yesterday, not just on features. You can't randomly shuffle your train/test split without creating data leakage.

Components of Time Series

  • Trend: long-term direction (increasing, decreasing, flat)
  • Seasonality: repeating patterns at fixed periods (daily, weekly, yearly)
  • Cyclicity: non-fixed period patterns (economic cycles)
  • Residual: random noise after removing trend + seasonality

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: Amazon forecasts demand for 10M+ SKUs across 200+ fulfillment centers. A 1% improvement in forecast accuracy saves $100M+ in inventory holding costs annually.

Classical Approaches

  • ARIMA (AutoRegressive Integrated Moving Average): AR(p) + differencing(d) + MA(q). p,d,q selected via ACF/PACF plots or grid search (auto.arima).
  • Exponential Smoothing (ETS): weighted average of past values, weights decay exponentially. Variants: Holt (trend), Holt-Winters (trend + seasonality).
  • Prophet (Meta): decomposes into trend (piecewise linear/logistic) + seasonality (Fourier series) + holidays. Handles missing data, outliers, and changepoints automatically.

ML Approaches for Time Series

  • Feature engineering: lag features (y_{t-1}, y_{t-2}), rolling statistics (7-day mean, 30-day std), time features (day of week, month, hour, holiday flag), Fourier features for seasonality
  • Gradient boosting (XGBoost/LightGBM): often outperforms pure time series models on forecasting competitions (M5 Accuracy competition winner used LightGBM with 200+ features)
  • Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCN): causal convolutions (only look backward), dilated convolutions for long-range dependencies
  • Encoder-Decoder (Seq2Seq): LSTM/GRU encoder reads history, decoder predicts future. Problem: error accumulation over long horizons.
  • Transformers for Time Series: Informer (ProbSparse attention), Autoformer (series decomposition), PatchTST (patch time series into tokens — SOTA for long-horizon forecasting)

Multi-Step Forecasting Strategies

  • Recursive: predict one step, feed prediction as input for next step. Error accumulates.
  • Direct: train separate model for each horizon. Stable but ignores temporal dependencies between horizons.
  • Sequence-to-Sequence: predict all horizons at once from an encoder. Best accuracy but most complex.
  • Rolling window: retrain periodically (daily/weekly) on a sliding window of recent data. Handles concept drift.

[Practical Workflow] Practical Workflow — Building a Forecast Model:

  1. Visualize the series — check for trend, seasonality, outliers, missing data
  2. Split chronologically (NO random shuffle). Typically: train=first 80% time period, dev=next 10%, test=last 10%
  3. Start with naive baseline (y_hat_{t} = y_{t-1}) — all models must beat this
  4. Try Prophet for quick baseline (handles holidays, changepoints automatically)
  5. Engineer lag/rolling/time features and try XGBoost
  6. If more data (>100K time steps), try TCN or Transformer
  7. Evaluate: MAE, RMSE, MAPE (for non-zero series). For multiple series (e.g., many products): SMAPE, MASE, WAPE

[Interview Angle] Interview Angle: Amazon L5 | Supply chain forecasting is core to Amazon. Expect questions on time-based CV (expanding window vs sliding window), handling multiple seasonal periods (daily + weekly), and cold-start forecasting for new products.
sections.codeBlock:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error

# --- Time series feature engineering for XGBoost ---
# Given a pandas Series `ts` with DatetimeIndex:

def create_lag_features(ts, lags=[1, 7, 14, 28]):
    """Create lag features for time series forecasting."""
    df = pd.DataFrame({'y': ts})
    for lag in lags:
        df[f'y_lag_{lag}'] = df['y'].shift(lag)
    return df

def create_rolling_features(ts, windows=[7, 14, 30]):
    """Create rolling statistics (mean, std, min, max)."""
    df = pd.DataFrame({'y': ts})
    for w in windows:
        df[f'y_rolling_mean_{w}'] = df['y'].rolling(w).mean()
        df[f'y_rolling_std_{w}'] = df['y'].rolling(w).std()
    return df

def create_time_features(ts):
    """Create calendar/time features from DateTimeIndex."""
    df = pd.DataFrame(index=ts.index)
    df['hour'] = df.index.hour
    df['day_of_week'] = df.index.dayofweek  # 0=Monday
    df['day_of_month'] = df.index.day
    df['month'] = df.index.month
    df['quarter'] = df.index.quarter
    df['is_weekend'] = (df.index.dayofweek >= 5).astype(int)
    return df

# Combine all features
# df = pd.concat([
#     create_lag_features(ts),
#     create_rolling_features(ts),
#     create_time_features(ts)
# ], axis=1)

# Chronological split (NO shuffle!)
# cutoff = int(len(df) * 0.8)
# train = df.iloc[:cutoff]
# test = df.iloc[cutoff:]

# X_train = train.drop('y', axis=1).dropna()
# y_train = train.loc[X_train.index, 'y']
# X_test = test.drop('y', axis=1).dropna()
# y_test = test.loc[X_test.index, 'y']

# XGBoost
# import xgboost as xgb
# model = xgb.XGBRegressor(n_estimators=1000, learning_rate=0.01, early_stopping_rounds=50)
# model.fit(X_train, y_train, eval_set=[(X_test, y_test)], verbose=False)
# preds = model.predict(X_test)
# print(f'MAE: {mean_absolute_error(y_test, preds):.4f}')

sections.title: FAANG Interview Q&A
sections.description:
[Interview Angle] Q1: "Derive the OLS estimator for linear regression." — Google L4

Start with the objective: L(w) = ||Xw - y||² = (Xw-y)ᵀ(Xw-y) = wᵀXᵀXw - 2wᵀXᵀy + yᵀy. Take gradient: ∇L = 2XᵀXw - 2Xᵀy. Set to zero: XᵀXw = Xᵀy. Solve: ŵ = (XᵀX)⁻¹Xᵀy. Key assumptions: X has full column rank (XᵀX invertible), and the model is correctly specified (E[ε|X] = 0).

[Interview Angle] Q2: "Why does L1 regularization give sparse solutions but L2 doesn't?" — Meta E5

The key is geometry. The regularized objective is the MSE plus constraint: Lasso = MSE + λ·Σ|wⱼ|, Ridge = MSE + λ·Σwⱼ². The constraint region for Lasso is a diamond (L1 ball), which has sharp corners at the axes. For a convex objective, the intersection with the constraint region will often occur at these corners — making one coefficient exactly zero. Ridge's constraint is a circle (L2 ball) — smooth, no corners. The intersection point is almost never exactly on an axis. By the KKT conditions, L1's subgradient at zero includes the [−λ, λ] interval, making zero a stable point.

[Interview Angle] Q3: "When would you use XGBoost over a neural network?" — Amazon L5

For tabular/structured data with < 100K samples, XGBoost typically outperforms neural networks. NNs need large data to learn representations; XGBoost works well with feature engineering. XGBoost handles missing values, mixed data types, and is easier to tune. Use NNs for images, text, audio, or when you have > 1M samples and want representation learning. In practice, AutoML systems often win with boosted trees on tabular data. The key tradeoff: XGBoost is a ready-made feature processor; NNs learn features from raw data.

[Interview Angle] Q4: "Explain the kernel trick and how RBF maps to infinite dimensions." — Google L5

The kernel trick: instead of computing φ(xᵢ)·φ(xⱼ) explicitly (which would be O(n²) in the feature space dimension), we compute K(xᵢ,xⱼ) directly. For RBF: K(x,y) = exp(-γ||x-y||²). Expand the exponential using Taylor series: exp(-γ||x-y||²) = Σₖ₌₀∞ (γ||x-y||²)ᵏ/k!. Each term involves polynomial features of degree k. After the binomial expansion, this reveals the RBF kernel corresponds to an infinite-dimensional feature vector containing all monomials of all orders, weighted by the Taylor coefficients. We never need to construct φ — just compute the kernel. This is why RBF can separate any finite set of points with enough γ (though it will overfit with γ too large).

[Interview Angle] Q5: "What's the difference between Bagging and Boosting?" — Apple E4

Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating) trains models in parallel on bootstrap samples and averages their predictions. It reduces variance without increasing bias. Random Forest is the canonical example. Boosting trains models sequentially, where each new model corrects the errors of the previous ensemble. It reduces bias (can fit complex patterns) but can increase variance if overfit. XGBoost is the canonical example. Key differences: Bagging is parallel (fast to train), uses deep trees (low bias each), reduces variance. Boosting is sequential (slower), uses shallow trees (high bias each), reduces bias. Bagging rarely overfits with more trees; boosting must use early stopping.

sections.title: References
sections.description:
• ESL (Hastie et al.) — Chapters 3-7: all supervised learning
  • XGBoost paper — Chen & Guestrin (2016): "XGBoost: A Scalable Tree Boosting System"
  • LightGBM paper — Ke et al. (2017): "LightGBM: A Highly Efficient Gradient Boosting Decision Tree"
  • CatBoost paper — Prokhorenkova et al. (2018): unbiased boosting with categorical features
  • sklearn documentation — ensemble, svm, neighbors modules
  • The Kernel Trick — Schölkopf & Smola: Learning with Kernels
